questions.question:
Kind : _______ : : Often : Frequently

(By word meaning)

A. Mean   B. Type   C. Cruel   D. Kindly
questions.to_find: All words that relate to 'Kind' in the same way 'Frequently' relates to 'Often' (synonym pairs)

understand.plain: This is an MSQ (multiple select) analogy using 'By word meaning'. The relationship is synonymy: Often and Frequently are synonyms. We must find ALL options that are synonyms of 'Kind'. 'Kind' has two relevant senses: as a noun meaning 'type/category' (synonym: Type) and as an adjective meaning 'gentle/benevolent' (related form: Kindly). Both B. Type and D. Kindly are valid synonyms of 'Kind' in its respective senses, making this an MSQ with two correct answers.

keywords.explain: A word with the same or nearly the same meaning as another word in the same language.
keywords.example: Often and Frequently are synonyms; Kind (noun) and Type are synonyms.

keywords.explain: A single word having multiple related meanings; 'Kind' means a type/category (noun) or gentle/caring (adjective).
keywords.example: 'What kind of bird?' uses Kind as a noun; 'She is a kind person' uses Kind as an adjective.

keywords.explain: A question type where more than one option may be correct; all correct options must be selected.
keywords.example: Both B. Type (noun synonym) and D. Kindly (adjective/adverb form) are synonyms of Kind.

understand.visual_alt: MSQ synonym analogy with Kind having two correct synonyms: Type (noun sense) and Kindly (adjective sense).

given.aim: Find all words that complete the synonym analogy Kind : _____ :: Often : Frequently, selecting all valid synonyms of Kind.

terms.meaning: Frequently; happening many times or in many cases.
terms.example: It often rains in monsoon season.
terms.connects: Paired with 'Frequently' to establish synonymy as the relationship.

terms.meaning: Synonym of 'Often'; at short intervals, many times repeatedly.
terms.example: She frequently visits the library.
terms.connects: Confirms the analogy is about synonyms; the blank must be a synonym of Kind.

terms.meaning: A category or type; a group of things sharing common traits.
terms.example: What kind of bird is that? (type/category)
terms.connects: The noun sense of Kind is synonymous with 'Type' (option B).

terms.meaning: Gentle, caring, and benevolent toward others.
terms.example: She is a kind and generous person.
terms.connects: The adjective sense relates to 'Kindly' (option D) which is a synonym/adverb form.

given.plan: Identify synonymy as the relationship; check each option against both the noun and adjective senses of 'Kind'; select all that qualify.

steps.title: Establish the relationship in the given pair
steps.apply: Often : Frequently -> these are synonyms. The relationship is synonymy; we need all synonyms of Kind from the options.
steps.note: This is MSQ; more than one answer can be correct.

steps.title: Evaluate each option as a synonym of Kind
steps.apply: A. Mean = average or unkind; not a synonym of Kind. B. Type = synonym of Kind (noun: a kind of thing = a type of thing). Correct. C. Cruel = antonym of Kind (adjective); not a synonym. D. Kindly = synonym/adjective-adverb form of Kind (gentle, benevolent). Correct. Selected: B and D.
steps.note: Both noun sense (Type) and adjective/adverb sense (Kindly) of Kind yield synonyms among the options, hence two correct answers.

questions.formula_note: This question uses no mathematical formulas; it tests recognition of synonym relationships and polysemy of 'Kind' in English.

questions.question:
Bird : Nest : : Bee : _______

A. Kennel   B. Hammock   C. Hive   D. Lair
questions.to_find: The dwelling/home of a Bee, analogous to Nest being the home of a Bird

understand.plain: This analogy is about animal dwellings. A Bird lives in a Nest. Similarly, we need to find where a Bee lives. A Bee lives in a Hive. The other options refer to homes of other animals: Kennel is for dogs, Lair is for wild animals like bears or wolves, and Hammock is not an animal dwelling at all.

keywords.explain: A structure in which bees live and store honey; the home/colony of bees.
keywords.example: The beekeeper carefully opened the hive to harvest honey.

keywords.explain: A structure built by a bird (or other animal) for laying eggs and raising young; its home.
keywords.example: The robin built a nest in the tree.

keywords.explain: A relationship where the second word is the habitat or home of the first word.
keywords.example: Dog : Kennel :: Horse : Stable :: Fish : Aquarium.

understand.visual_alt: Analogy diagram showing Bird to Nest and Bee to Hive as creature-to-home pairs.

given.aim: Identify the home of a Bee, using the Bird : Nest relationship as a template (creature : dwelling).

terms.meaning: A feathered, winged vertebrate animal.
terms.example: A sparrow is a small bird.
terms.connects: Anchor word; its dwelling is Nest.

terms.meaning: A bird's home, typically made of twigs and grass, used for laying and incubating eggs.
terms.example: The mother bird returned to her nest with food.
terms.connects: Establishes the creature-to-dwelling relationship.

terms.meaning: A flying insect known for collecting nectar, making honey, and living in colonies.
terms.example: Bees are vital pollinators for many crops.
terms.connects: The creature whose dwelling we must find.

terms.meaning: A man-made box or natural cavity where a colony of bees lives; also called beehive.
terms.example: Thousands of worker bees live in a single hive.
terms.connects: The correct dwelling for Bee, completing the analogy.

given.plan: Match the relationship Creature : Dwelling; identify the correct dwelling for Bee among the options by eliminating wrong animal homes.

steps.title: Identify the relationship in the given pair
steps.apply: Bird : Nest => the relationship is creature to its home/dwelling.
steps.note: This is a straightforward creature-to-habitat analogy.

steps.title: Match each option to Bee's correct dwelling
steps.apply: A. Kennel = home of a dog. B. Hammock = a suspended bed, not an animal home. C. Hive = home of a bee colony (correct). D. Lair = den of a wild animal (lion, bear, etc.). Therefore C. Hive is correct.
steps.note: All distractors refer to genuine animal homes but for different animals, testing vocabulary of animal dwellings.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a vocabulary-based creature-to-dwelling analogy.

questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:

Cover : Uncover : : Associate : _______

A. Unassociate   B. Inassociate   C. Misassociate   D. Dissociate
questions.to_find: The antonym of 'Associate' formed by an appropriate prefix, analogous to 'Uncover' being the antonym of 'Cover'

understand.plain: The relationship here is word : antonym-by-prefix. 'Uncover' is the standard opposite of 'Cover' using the prefix 'un-'. We need the standard antonym of 'Associate'. While 'un-' works for many verbs (uncover, undo, untie), the standard established opposite of 'associate' is 'dissociate' using the prefix 'dis-'. The other options (Unassociate, Inassociate, Misassociate) are not standard English words.

keywords.explain: Reversal prefix used with many verbs to form antonyms (undo, unlock, uncover).
keywords.example: Cover -> Uncover; Lock -> Unlock; Wrap -> Unwrap.

keywords.explain: A prefix meaning reversal or negation, used to form antonyms of certain words (dissociate, disconnect, disagree).
keywords.example: Associate -> Dissociate; Connect -> Disconnect; Agree -> Disagree.

keywords.explain: To disconnect or separate from an association or relationship; the established antonym of 'associate'.
keywords.example: She decided to dissociate herself from the controversial group.

understand.visual_alt: Word analogy showing Cover-to-Uncover and Associate-to-Dissociate as prefix-reversal pairs.

given.aim: Find the correctly prefixed antonym of 'Associate' following the pattern Cover : Uncover (verb : its opposite via prefix).

terms.meaning: To place something over or upon in order to conceal or protect.
terms.example: Please cover the food to keep it fresh.
terms.connects: Base word; its antonym is Uncover.

terms.meaning: To remove a cover from; to reveal or expose; antonym of Cover.
terms.example: The archaeologists uncovered an ancient artifact.
terms.connects: Establishes the base-word to prefix-antonym relationship.

terms.meaning: To connect or bring into relation; to join with others in a group or activity.
terms.example: People associate the color red with danger.
terms.connects: Base word whose antonym we must find.

terms.meaning: To disconnect or separate from association; the established antonym of associate (using prefix 'dis-').
terms.example: He tried to dissociate his personal life from his work.
terms.connects: Correct antonym of Associate, completing the analogy.

given.plan: Identify that the relationship is 'word : antonym by prefix'; determine which prefix forms a real English word as the antonym of Associate; eliminate non-words.

steps.title: Identify the prefix-antonym pattern
steps.apply: Cover -> Uncover uses prefix 'un-' for reversal. The pattern is: word -> prefix + word forming a legitimate antonym.
steps.note: The analogy requires a standard, established English word, not just any prefix attachment.

steps.title: Test each option against Associate
steps.apply: A. Unassociate: not a standard dictionary word. B. Inassociate: not a standard word. C. Misassociate: 'mis-' means wrongly, not reversal; not standard. D. Dissociate: standard English word meaning to separate or disconnect; the accepted antonym of associate. Answer: D.
steps.note: The prefix 'dis-' commonly creates antonyms for words already containing a Latin stem (associate from Latin associare), whereas 'un-' works for native English verbs like 'cover'.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this question tests English prefix usage and standard antonym formation.

questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:

Fuse : Fusion : : Use : _______

A. Usage   B. User   C. Uses   D. Usion
questions.to_find: The noun form of 'Use' that parallels how 'Fusion' is derived from 'Fuse'

understand.plain: The relationship is between a verb and its corresponding abstract noun. 'Fuse' (verb) gives rise to 'Fusion' (noun of action/process). Similarly, 'Use' (verb) should give rise to an abstract noun. 'Usage' is the abstract noun form of 'Use' that refers to the manner or act of using something. 'User' is an agent noun (one who uses), 'Uses' is the third-person plural/verb form, and 'Usion' is not a word.

keywords.explain: Abstract noun derived from the verb 'fuse'; the process or result of joining or melting together.
keywords.example: Nuclear fusion releases enormous energy.

keywords.explain: Abstract noun derived from the verb 'use'; the manner in which something is used, or the established practice of using it.
keywords.example: The usage of smartphones has increased dramatically.

keywords.explain: The morphological process of forming nouns from verbs, often via suffixes like -ion, -age, -ment, etc.
keywords.example: Act -> Action; Use -> Usage; Move -> Movement.

understand.visual_alt: Word analogy showing Fuse-to-Fusion and Use-to-Usage as verb-to-abstract-noun pairs.

given.aim: Identify the abstract noun form of 'Use' that mirrors the relationship Fuse : Fusion (verb : abstract noun).

terms.meaning: To join, blend, or melt together; also a safety device in electrical circuits.
terms.example: The two wires fuse under intense heat.
terms.connects: Base verb whose noun form is Fusion.

terms.meaning: The abstract noun of 'Fuse'; the process of joining or merging into a single entity.
terms.example: Jazz fusion blends multiple musical styles.
terms.connects: Establishes the verb-to-abstract-noun relationship.

terms.meaning: To employ or apply something for a purpose; to put into service.
terms.example: Please use the correct tool for each job.
terms.connects: Base verb whose abstract noun we must find.

terms.meaning: The manner or extent to which something is used; established practice or custom in using something.
terms.example: The textbook discusses the usage of prepositions in English.
terms.connects: The abstract noun of 'Use', completing the analogy.

given.plan: Confirm the relationship (verb : its abstract/process noun), then identify which option is the genuine abstract noun derived from 'Use'.

steps.title: Confirm the relationship in Fuse : Fusion
steps.apply: Fuse (verb) -> Fusion (noun); the suffix '-ion' creates an abstract noun indicating a process or state. The relationship is: verb -> abstract noun of the process.
steps.note: Both words come from Latin root 'fundere/fusus'; 'fusion' is the noun form.

steps.title: Find the equivalent noun for 'Use'
steps.apply: A. Usage = noun indicating the act or manner of using (correct abstract noun). B. User = agent noun (person who uses), not a process noun. C. Uses = 3rd person singular verb or plural noun, not an abstract process noun. D. Usion = not a real word. Therefore A. Usage matches the pattern.
steps.note: Note that 'Use' can also be a noun itself, but 'Usage' is the specific abstract noun paralleling 'Fusion' more closely in morphological derivation.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this question tests English morphological derivation (verb-to-noun suffix patterns).

questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:

Partial : Impartial : : Popular : _______

A. Impopular   B. Dispopular   C. Mispopular   D. Unpopular
questions.to_find: The correct antonym of 'Popular' formed analogously to how 'Impartial' is the antonym of 'Partial'

understand.plain: This analogy involves forming antonyms with negative prefixes. 'Impartial' is the antonym of 'Partial', using the prefix 'im-' (a variant of 'in-' used before words starting with 'p'). For 'Popular', we must pick the correctly established antonym from the options. While one might guess 'impopular' by the same logic, the standard English antonym of 'popular' is 'unpopular', not 'impopular'. English prefix selection depends on the specific word's etymology and established usage.

keywords.explain: Variant of prefix 'in-' (meaning not) used before words beginning with p, b, or m (impossible, impartial, immature).
keywords.example: Partial -> Impartial; Possible -> Impossible; Moral -> Immoral.

keywords.explain: Negative prefix of Germanic origin; widely used in English to negate adjectives (unhappy, unpopular, unkind).
keywords.example: Popular -> Unpopular; Happy -> Unhappy; Kind -> Unkind.

keywords.explain: The standard established antonym of 'popular'; not liked or approved of by many people.
keywords.example: The new tax was deeply unpopular with the public.

understand.visual_alt: Word analogy showing Partial-to-Impartial and Popular-to-Unpopular as word-to-established-antonym pairs.

given.aim: Find the standard, established antonym of 'Popular' using prefix negation, following the Partial : Impartial pattern.

terms.meaning: Favouring one side unfairly; not complete or whole.
terms.example: The referee was accused of being partial toward the home team.
terms.connects: Base word; its antonym is Impartial.

terms.meaning: Treating all people equally without favouritism; fair and unbiased.
terms.example: A judge must remain impartial during proceedings.
terms.connects: Establishes the prefix-negation antonym relationship.

terms.meaning: Liked or admired by many people; widely accepted or favoured.
terms.example: The singer became popular after winning the talent show.
terms.connects: Base word whose antonym we must find.

terms.meaning: Not liked or supported by many people; the standard antonym of popular.
terms.example: The decision to close the park was unpopular with residents.
terms.connects: The correct established antonym of Popular in English usage.

given.plan: Confirm the relationship (word : established antonym via negative prefix), then identify the correctly established English antonym of Popular from the options.

steps.title: Confirm the relationship: word to established antonym via negative prefix
steps.apply: Partial -> Impartial: the prefix 'im-' (variant of 'in-') negates 'partial'. The pattern requires a standard dictionary antonym.
steps.note: The analogy asks for the established English word, not just any grammatically possible prefix attachment.

steps.title: Eliminate non-standard options and confirm the answer
steps.apply: A. Impopular: although 'im-' is used before 'p', 'impopular' is NOT a standard English word. B. Dispopular: 'dis-' is incorrect here; not a standard word. C. Mispopular: 'mis-' means wrongly, not suitable for negation; not standard. D. Unpopular: the accepted, established antonym of 'popular' in standard English dictionaries. Answer: D.
steps.note: This question tests whether students know the correct established English word rather than mechanically applying the same prefix 'im-' from the model pair.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this question tests knowledge of established English antonyms and prefix usage patterns.

questions.question:
Eject : Insert : : Advance : _________

(By word meaning)

A. Advent   B. Progress   C. Retreat   D. Loan
questions.to_find: The word that is the antonym of 'Advance', analogous to 'Insert' being the antonym of 'Eject'

understand.plain: The relationship between Eject and Insert is that of antonyms: to eject means to push/throw out, while to insert means to put in. Both describe opposite movements with respect to a container or boundary. Similarly, we need the antonym of Advance. Advance means to move forward, while Retreat means to move backward or withdraw. This matches the opposite-movement relationship exactly.

keywords.explain: To push or force out; to expel something from inside to outside.
keywords.example: The DVD player ejects the disc when you press the button.

keywords.explain: To put into; the direct opposite of eject (something goes in rather than out).
keywords.example: Insert the key into the lock to open the door.

keywords.explain: To move back or withdraw from a position; the antonym of advance.
keywords.example: Faced with superior forces, the army was forced to retreat.

understand.visual_alt: Antonym analogy diagram: Eject (out) paired with Insert (in), and Advance (forward) paired with Retreat (backward).

given.aim: Identify the antonym of 'Advance' using the same antonym relationship as Eject : Insert (by word meaning).

terms.meaning: To forcibly push or throw out; to cause to leave a position.
terms.example: The pilot had to eject when the aircraft lost control.
terms.connects: Anchor word; its antonym is Insert.

terms.meaning: To place or put something into something else; direct opposite of Eject.
terms.example: Insert a coin to start the machine.
terms.connects: Establishes the antonym relationship for the analogy.

terms.meaning: To move forward; to make progress toward a goal or position.
terms.example: The troops advanced toward the city.
terms.connects: Base word whose antonym we must identify.

terms.meaning: To move back or withdraw from a position; the direct antonym of advance.
terms.example: The army had to retreat when outnumbered.
terms.connects: Correct antonym of Advance, completing the analogy.

given.plan: Confirm the relationship is antonymy (by word meaning), then identify the true antonym of Advance from the options, eliminating synonyms and unrelated words.

steps.title: Confirm the relationship: Eject : Insert are antonyms
steps.apply: Eject = to push out; Insert = to put in. These are opposites. Relationship: antonymy (by word meaning).
steps.note: The question explicitly says 'By word meaning', confirming we are looking for meaning-based opposites.

steps.title: Find the antonym of Advance
steps.apply: A. Advent = arrival/coming; not an antonym of advance. B. Progress = moving forward; this is a synonym of advance, not an antonym. C. Retreat = moving backward; direct antonym of advance. D. Loan = lending money; unrelated. Answer: C. Retreat.
steps.note: Progress is a common distractor since it sounds like it might oppose 'advance', but they are actually synonyms both meaning to move forward or improve.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this question tests identification of antonym pairs by word meaning.

questions.question:
Kind : _________ : : Often : Seldom

(By word meaning)

A. Cruel   B. Variety   C. Type   D. Kindred
questions.to_find: The antonym of 'Kind' (adjective) that parallels 'Seldom' being the antonym of 'Often'

understand.plain: In this analogy, the relationship between Often and Seldom is antonymy: Often means frequently, Seldom means rarely. They are direct opposites. We need the antonym of 'Kind' used as an adjective (gentle, caring, benevolent). The opposite of being kind is being Cruel. Note: this is a different question from 11.22.9 where Kind was used as a noun (synonym of Type). Here, by the antonym relationship established via Often : Seldom, Kind (adjective) : Cruel is the correct pair.

keywords.explain: Frequently, many times; happening in a large proportion of cases.
keywords.example: It often rains in the mountains.

keywords.explain: Rarely, not often; the direct antonym of Often.
keywords.example: He seldom misses a deadline.

keywords.explain: Causing pain or suffering; lacking compassion; the antonym of Kind (adjective).
keywords.example: It is cruel to mistreat animals.

understand.visual_alt: Antonym analogy: Often-Seldom (frequency opposites) and Kind-Cruel (behavior/character opposites).

given.aim: Identify the antonym of 'Kind' (adjective: gentle/benevolent) using the Often : Seldom antonym relationship as the template.

terms.meaning: Frequently; in a large number of situations.
terms.example: She often reads before bedtime.
terms.connects: Anchor word; its antonym is Seldom.

terms.meaning: Rarely; not often; on few occasions.
terms.example: He seldom watches television.
terms.connects: Establishes antonymy as the relationship.

terms.meaning: Having a friendly, generous, and considerate nature; gentle and caring toward others.
terms.example: She was kind enough to help the lost tourist.
terms.connects: Base adjective whose antonym we must find.

terms.meaning: Causing pain or suffering willfully; devoid of compassion; direct antonym of Kind (adjective).
terms.example: Cruelty toward others is never justified.
terms.connects: Correct antonym of Kind (adjective), completing the analogy.

given.plan: Confirm the relationship is antonymy (Often <-> Seldom); then find the antonym of Kind as an adjective, being careful not to confuse the noun sense of Kind (= Type).

steps.title: Confirm antonym relationship from Often : Seldom
steps.apply: Often (frequently) : Seldom (rarely) are antonyms. The relationship is antonymy. We need the antonym of Kind.
steps.note: This is the key distinction from 11.22.9 which used a synonym relationship. Here Often : Seldom are opposites, so we need the opposite of Kind.

steps.title: Identify the antonym of Kind (adjective)
steps.apply: Kind (adjective) = gentle, benevolent, caring. A. Cruel = causing pain, harsh, the direct antonym of Kind (adjective). B. Variety = range of types; unrelated. C. Type = synonym of Kind (noun); not an antonym. D. Kindred = related or similar; not an antonym. Answer: A. Cruel.
steps.note: Contrast with question 11.22.9 where Kind : Type was correct because Often : Frequently were synonyms. Here Often : Seldom are antonyms, so the answer shifts to the adjective antonym A. Cruel.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this question tests antonym identification for adjectives and careful reading of the relationship type in word pair analogies.
